common_name: Gulf Crimson Peach (UF IFAS Low-Chill Freestone Peach for the Gulf South)

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter (January–February in zone 9) while dormant; Gulf Crimson requires approximately 300–350 chilling hours (hours below 45°F), which is reliably met in zone 8–9 most winters. Bloom occurs January–February in zone 9 and late frost can damage blossoms — plant in areas with good cold-air drainage to minimize frost pocket risk. Gulf Crimson is a freestone peach with yellow flesh and red skin that ripens in late April through May in the Gulf South, making it one of the earliest-ripening peach options and providing fresh peaches 4–6 weeks before later-season varieties. Thin fruit to 4–6 inch spacing when walnut-sized for maximum fruit size.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in full sun, well-drained soil; stake young trees and train to an open-center form. Thin fruit aggressively in the first weeks of fruit set for maximum size. Gulf Crimson is one of the University of Florida IFAS releases from the peach breeding program that has made commercial and home peach production possible throughout the Gulf South — a region where most standard peach varieties fail because they require 700–1,200 chilling hours that the mild Gulf South winters cannot provide. Gulf Crimson's low chill requirement (300–350 hours) means that even in the warmest parts of zone 8–9, the tree accumulates adequate winter dormancy to bloom and set fruit reliably each year. The fruit is medium to large-sized, with attractively red-blushed skin over a yellow-green ground color, and yellow flesh that is sweet, aromatic, and freestone — the pit separates cleanly from the flesh at ripeness. Gulf Crimson's early ripening (late April to May in zone 9) means the harvest precedes the peak summer heat that stresses trees and reduces fruit quality in later-season varieties. The University of Florida peach breeding program (which also produced Gulf Gold, Gulf King, and Gulfrose) developed Gulf Crimson specifically for the low-chill peach market across zone 7–9 of the Gulf Coast and Atlantic Coastal Plain.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Low-Chill Peach Variety Development and Gulf Crimson notes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Low-Chill Peach Varieties for the Gulf South
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Peach Variety Recommendations for Louisiana

common_name: Acerola Cherry (Barbados Cherry, Highest Vitamin C Natural Tropical Fruit)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or nursery plants in spring after last frost in full sun; acerola is a frost-sensitive tropical shrub that is damaged below 28°F and killed by temperatures below 25°F. In zone 9, plant against a south-facing wall for frost protection, in containers that can be moved indoors, or in frost-protected microclimates. Acerola produces 3 or more fruit crops per year in frost-free conditions — the plant blooms and fruits almost continuously in warm, humid conditions. The fruits should be harvested at full red ripeness and consumed or processed immediately — acerola loses Vitamin C rapidly after harvest (50% within 24 hours at room temperature). Stem cuttings root readily.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in full sun, well-drained soil; protect from frost. Prune lightly after harvest to maintain shape and encourage new fruiting wood. Acerola Cherry (Malpighia emarginata, Barbados Cherry, West Indian Cherry) is a tropical American shrub producing small, bright red, cherry-shaped fruits with the highest Vitamin C content of any commonly cultivated fruit — measured at 1,600–4,700 mg per 100 grams of fresh fruit, compared to 50–90 mg per 100 grams in oranges. This extraordinary Vitamin C concentration — 30–100 times that of citrus — makes acerola one of the most nutritionally significant fruits available to home gardeners in zone 9–11, where the plant is well-adapted to the heat and humidity of the Gulf South. The fruits are small (0.5–1 inch), three-lobed, brilliantly red at ripeness, with soft, juicy, somewhat tart flesh — the tartness masks the sweetness but reflects the very high ascorbic acid content. Acerola fruit has extremely limited commercial availability in North America because the ripe fruits are too soft and perishable for commercial shipping; the best quality fruit is available only to gardeners who can harvest and consume them immediately. Acerola is used for fresh eating, juicing, smoothies, and is commercially important in South America (especially Brazil) for vitamin supplement production from the concentrated juice.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Acerola Cherry Production in Florida
Purdue University — Horticulture and Landscape Architecture: Acerola (Malpighia emarginata) overview
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Vitamin C content of tropical fruits

common_name: White Marseilles Fig (Lattarula Italian Honey Fig, Sweet Golden-Fleshed Mediterranean Fig)

planting.note: Plant cuttings or nursery plants in spring in full sun; White Marseilles (also sold as 'Lattarula,' 'Italian Honey Fig,' and 'Blanche') produces fruits with very thin, tender, pale green to yellow-green skin that turns translucent at full ripeness. In zone 9, White Marseilles produces a breba (early) crop on old wood in June and a main crop on new wood in August–September. The thin, tender skin is a premium eating quality attribute but also a minor limitation for handling — harvest gently and consume quickly. Protect from frost below 18–20°F when young; established plants tolerate brief periods to 15°F.

directions: Plant nursery trees or rooted cuttings in full sun, well-drained soil; prune in late winter to shape. White Marseilles is one of the oldest documented fig varieties in European cultivation — referenced in 17th-century English garden literature and grown continuously in Mediterranean European gardens for centuries under various local names including Lattarula (Italian, meaning little bottle), Blanche (French), and White Marseilles (English, after the port city of Marseilles from which it was widely distributed). The fruit is medium to large, turbinate (pear-shaped) to round, with pale green to yellowish-green skin that becomes translucent amber-green at full ripeness — visually distinctive from the dark-skinned black and brown figs that are the most common fig types sold in American nurseries. The flesh is pale amber, extremely sweet and rich with a honey-like quality — the Lattarula name acknowledges the syrupy honey character of the ripe fruit — and the flavor is described as one of the finest among common fig varieties, with a complex sweetness that surpasses the simpler flavor of standard commercial brown turkey types. White Marseilles performs well in the warm, long growing seasons of zone 8–10, where the extended summer gives the main crop time to fully develop the honey concentration that distinguishes it.
sources: California Rare Fruit Growers — White Marseilles / Lattarula fig variety notes
University of California Cooperative Extension — Fig Varieties for California and Similar Climates
Specialty Fruit Growers Association — Heirloom fig variety documentation

common_name: Morris Plum (Texas A&M Low-Chill Self-Fertile Japanese Plum for the Gulf South)

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter (January–February) while dormant; Morris requires approximately 400–450 chilling hours, suitable for most of zone 7–9. Morris is one of the few Japanese plums reliably self-fertile — it can set a good crop without a pollinator, though planting a second Japanese plum (Methley, Bruce, Santa Rosa) will improve fruit set and crop size. Fruit ripens late May through June in zone 9 — earlier than most Japanese plums. Thin fruit to 3–4 inch spacing at marble size for maximum fruit development.

directions: Plant bare-root trees in late winter in full sun, well-drained soil; prune to open-center form. Thin fruit heavily at marble size for maximum size and quality. Morris Plum is a Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) developed at the Texas A&M University Agricultural Experiment Station in Montague County, Texas — one of the primary low-chill Japanese plum varieties released specifically for the hot, humid climate of the South and Gulf Coast region where most standard Japanese plum varieties from California require too many chilling hours or fail to set fruit reliably. Morris carries a low chilling requirement (400–450 hours) that is reliably met throughout most of zone 7–9, and the variety's self-fertile nature (it can pollinate itself and set a full crop without a second plum nearby) makes it particularly practical for home gardeners with space for only one tree. The fruit is medium-sized, round, with mahogany-red skin and amber to yellow flesh — freestone at full ripeness — with good sweet-tart plum flavor. Morris is notably productive for a low-chill plum, bearing heavily in most years when the chilling requirement is met. Texas A&M's stone fruit breeding program (which also produced Bruce plum and other Gulf South stone fruit varieties) made commercial and home plum production viable across zone 7–9 of Texas and the surrounding Gulf South.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Morris plum development and Gulf South stone fruit notes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Plum Variety Recommendations for the Gulf South
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Japanese Plum Production in the Southeast

common_name: Shawnee Blackberry (University of Arkansas Large Late-Season Thorned Blackberry)

planting.note: Plant bare-root canes or rooted tip cuttings in late winter or spring; Shawnee is an erect, thorned blackberry producing canes 5–7 feet tall that benefit from a T-trellis for support of the heavy fruit load. In zone 9, plant January–February; canes fruit in their second year (first-year canes are vegetative; second-year floricanes bear fruit in June–July then die back). Shawnee ripens in late June–July in zone 9 — 1–2 weeks after earlier erect varieties — extending the harvest window. The thorns on Shawnee are significant and protective gloves are needed for pruning and harvest.

directions: Plant bare-root canes 3–4 feet apart in rows 8–10 feet apart; tip-prune new canes at 3–4 feet in summer to encourage lateral branching. After fruiting, remove second-year (fruited) canes at ground level. Shawnee is a University of Arkansas erect blackberry released in 1985 — part of the legendary Arkansas blackberry breeding program led by Dr. James N. Moore that produced the majority of the blackberry varieties grown commercially and in home gardens across the eastern United States. The Arkansas program's systematic approach to blackberry improvement — selecting for large fruit size, good flavor, and erect cane habit suitable for mechanical harvesting — transformed blackberry production from a minor cottage-scale enterprise to a significant commercial crop. Shawnee produces very large, firm, glossy black berries (among the largest of the Arkansas releases in its era) with excellent flavor — described as rich and sweet with good balance, not overly tart. The late-season ripening of Shawnee means the fruits develop during the hottest part of the Gulf South summer (June–July in zone 9), and adequate irrigation during fruit development is important to maintain size and quality. The erect cane habit (no sprawling) makes Shawnee manageable in home gardens with a simple 2-wire T-trellis at 3 and 5 feet, providing a contained, productive planting in moderate space.
sources: University of Arkansas Division of Agriculture — Shawnee blackberry development and variety notes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Blackberry Variety Recommendations for the Gulf South
North Carolina State University Extension — Southeastern Blackberry Production Guide
